title: The Fingerprint Verse
_note: Vertical 9:16 short (~80s). Pillar: Qur'an and creation (Tuesday). Authentic: Qur'an 75:3-4 (Al-Qiyamah) shown on screen, never recited. Science separate: Galton, 'Finger Prints' (1892). VISUALS MATCH THE SCENE: the footage is a code-generated fingerprint (concentric friction ridges + delta) on the dark-green/gold brand palette, NOT abstract stock. Human-free and text-free by construction (no Pexels QC), and each beat's ridge pattern is chosen to match its line (forms / core spotlight / crumble to dust / restore / Galton scan / two different prints for twins / one ridge as a signature / a pressed seal / gathered again from dust). Captions in the vertical safe area (clear of the right icon rail).

segments.text: Look closely at the tip of your finger. The pattern on it belongs to you, and to no one else who has ever lived.
Fourteen hundred years ago, the Qur'an pointed to this exact part of the body, while answering a man who doubted he could ever be brought back to life.
He asked, in effect: how could God ever gather my bones once I have crumbled into dust?
Yes. We are able to restore even the very tips of his fingers.
Of every part of the body it could have named, it named the fingertips. And in 1892, a scientist named Francis Galton showed why that is remarkable.
He proved that no two fingerprints are ever the same. Not between strangers. Not even between identical twins.
Every ridge is a signature that was never issued twice in all of human history.
So when the Qur'an singled out the fingertips, it pointed to the one small mark that makes every single person unmistakable.
The One who can tell you apart by a fingerprint can surely gather you again from dust. That was the whole point.
Reflect on the signs hidden in your own hands.

sources: Qur'an 75:3-4 (Al-Qiyamah), shown with translation, never recited: 'Does man think that We will not assemble his bones? Yes, We are Able to proportion his fingertips (bananah).' Arabic from Quran.com.
Science (separate): Sir Francis Galton, 'Finger Prints' (1892) established the uniqueness and lifelong permanence of fingerprint ridge patterns, including between identical twins.

Framing: reflection on a sign, no coded-miracle overclaim, no date-setting. Visuals are code-generated fingerprint ridges (no stock footage), so they match the subject exactly and are human-free / text-free by construction; captions kept inside the vertical safe area.
